summary: This PR refactors `HTTPDigestAuth` to store per-request state in `threading.local` rather than directly on `self`, aiming for thread safety. However, the implementation has critical correctness and security flaws: the `num_401_calls` field is initialized to `None` (instead of a safe integer), hook callbacks may execute on a different thread than the one that called `__call__` (leaving thread-local state completely uninitialized), and stale nonce/challenge state is never reset between requests on a reused thread. Test coverage for the new behavior is also insufficient, with the main new test silently becoming a no-op when `ThreadPool` is unavailable.

findings.title: num_401_calls initialized to None and hook callbacks may run on a different thread — both cause crashes
findings.detail:
Two related critical issues stem from the thread-local design:

1. **None initialization**: In `init_per_thread_state` (line 78), `self.tl.num_401_calls` is set to `None`. In `__call__`, it is only reset to `1` at line 221 — *after* hooks are already registered (lines 219–220). If `handle_401` or `handle_redirect` fires before `__call__` sets the value, `num_401_calls < 2` (line 180) raises `TypeError` in Python 3, and `self.tl.num_401_calls += 1` (line 182) also fails. The original code used `getattr(self, 'num_401_calls', 1)` as a safe fallback; the new code removes that safety net. The initial value should be `1` (not `None`) and/or the reset should occur before hook registration.

2. **Cross-thread callback dispatch**: Because `threading.local` state is per-thread, `handle_401` and `handle_redirect` must run on the same thread that called `__call__`. In async or thread-pooled HTTP stacks, response callbacks may be dispatched on a *different* thread, which will have no `init` attribute on `self.tl`. Since `init_per_thread_state` is never called in the hook path, every `self.tl` attribute access in the callbacks will raise `AttributeError`, completely bypassing the 401-loop guard and crashing authentication. The design assumption that the calling thread and callback thread are the same must be explicitly documented or enforced.

findings.title: test_DIGESTAUTH_THREADED silently skips entire assertion block when ThreadPool is unavailable
findings.detail: The `if ThreadPool is not None:` guard wraps only the pool usage at the bottom of the test function, so when `multiprocessing.pool.ThreadPool` cannot be imported the test passes vacuously without exercising anything. There is no `pytest.skip` or `@pytest.mark.skipif`, making the skip invisible in CI and providing false confidence. Either move the guard to the very top of the test or replace it with an explicit `pytest.skip` so the absence of `ThreadPool` is surfaced.

findings.title: No unit test for per-thread state isolation or num_401_calls reset behavior
findings.detail: `init_per_thread_state` initializes `tl.last_nonce`, `tl.nonce_count`, `tl.chal`, `tl.pos`, and `tl.num_401_calls` in thread-local storage, and `__call__` unconditionally resets `num_401_calls` to `1`. Neither behavior is tested in isolation. The only new test (`test_DIGESTAUTH_THREADED`) fires 100 concurrent requests and only checks the Authorization header value — it never verifies that two threads get independent state (e.g. independent `last_nonce`, `nonce_count`, or `num_401_calls`), nor that sequential calls on the same thread correctly reset the counter. A regression where state leaks between threads or the reset logic breaks would go undetected.

findings.title: Stale per-thread nonce/challenge state never reset between requests on the same thread
findings.detail: The guard `if not hasattr(self.tl, 'init')` in `init_per_thread_state` ensures initialization only happens once per thread. When a thread from a pool is reused for a second, independent request with the same `HTTPDigestAuth` object, `tl.last_nonce`, `tl.chal`, `tl.nonce_count`, and `tl.pos` retain their values from the previous request. `__call__` resets `num_401_calls` to `1` (line 221) but does NOT reset `last_nonce` or `chal`, so a reused thread will attempt to build a digest header with a potentially stale nonce/chal (lines 209–210), sending an Authorization header without a fresh 401 handshake that the server may reject. The correct fix is to reset per-thread state at the start of each request cycle in `__call__`.

findings.title: Attribute name `tl` is too abbreviated and opaque
findings.detail: The name `tl` gives no hint that it is a thread-local storage object. A name such as `_thread_local` or `_local` would be consistent with the surrounding verbosity and immediately communicate its purpose to readers unfamiliar with the codebase.

findings.title: Sentinel attribute `init` in init_per_thread_state is misleadingly named
findings.detail: The sentinel `self.tl.init = True` uses the name `init`, which reads like an action, but it is really a boolean guard flag. A name like `_initialized` would be much clearer about its role as a one-time initialization marker, consistent with the project's use of descriptive names.

findings.title: test_DIGESTAUTH_THREADED does not assert HTTP 200 status code
findings.detail: The test only checks that `'"auth"' in r.request.headers['Authorization']` but does not assert `r.status_code == 200`. A concurrent authentication failure that still produces an Authorization header (e.g. a 401 response carrying a stale header from a prior attempt) would not be caught, meaning the test could pass even when authentication is silently failing.

findings.title: 'Already initialized' path in init_per_thread_state is not tested
findings.detail: The guard `if not hasattr(self.tl, 'init')` causes the initialization block to be skipped on the second call from the same thread. There is no test that calls `__call__` twice from the same thread and asserts that state accumulated in the first call is preserved rather than reset. This branch could silently regress.

findings.title: handle_redirect() per-thread num_401_calls reset not covered by concurrent test
findings.detail: `handle_redirect` now resets `self.tl.num_401_calls = 1` using thread-local state. No test exercises this code path with multiple concurrent threads each following a redirect, so the interaction between the redirect reset and per-thread isolation is untested.

findings.title: No concurrent test for wrong-password path in threaded digest auth
findings.detail: `test_DIGESTAUTH_WRONG_HTTP_401_GET` covers the wrong-password 401-loop guard only serially. There is no concurrent version, so the thread-safety of the `num_401_calls < 2` loop-prevention logic — the primary motivation for this PR — is not exercised under a wrong-credentials scenario. A race condition in the loop guard would go undetected.

findings.title: Server-controlled www-authenticate header stored in long-lived thread-local without strict validation
findings.detail: The `www-authenticate` header value is parsed and stored in `tl.chal` without strict validation. Because `tl.chal` is now thread-scoped and survives across requests on the same thread (see stale-state finding), a malicious server could inject crafted values into `tl.chal` that persist and influence Authorization header construction (lines 152–162) for subsequent requests on the same thread. While not new to this PR, the thread-local refactor extends the lifetime of potentially attacker-influenced state.

findings.title: Missing spaces around `=` in keyword assignment
findings.detail: `session.auth=auth` is missing spaces around the `=`. All other assignments in the test file and the codebase use `session.auth = auth` style. This is a minor PEP 8 violation.

findings.title: `do_request` ignores its argument and returns an unused literal
findings.detail: The inner function `do_request(i)` never uses `i` and returns the literal `1` which is never inspected by callers. The unused parameter could be renamed to `_` to signal it is intentionally ignored, and `return 1` can be omitted to reduce noise.

findings.title: Per-thread state initialized only once, preventing reuse of same thread with a fresh digest challenge
findings.detail: `init_per_thread_state` sets `self.tl.init = True` on first call and never re-initializes. This means if the same thread is reused (e.g. from a thread pool) for a second, independent sequence of requests using the same `HTTPDigestAuth` object, `last_nonce`, `chal`, `nonce_count`, and `pos` retain their values from the previous request. While `__call__` does reset `num_401_calls` to 1 (line 221), it does NOT reset `last_nonce` or `chal`, so a reused thread will immediately attempt to build a digest header with a potentially stale nonce/chal on line 209-210 (`if self.tl.last_nonce:`). This can cause the auth to send a stale digest header without first going through a fresh 401 handshake, which the server may reject.

findings.title: `num_401_calls` is initialized to `None`; comparison `None < 2` raises TypeError in Python 3
findings.detail: In `init_per_thread_state` (line 78), `self.tl.num_401_calls` is set to `None`. In `__call__` (line 221) it is reset to `1` after registering hooks. However, if `handle_redirect` is triggered on a redirect response before `__call__` has had a chance to set `num_401_calls = 1` on this thread (e.g., in a pathological session/hook ordering), `handle_redirect` sets `self.tl.num_401_calls = 1`, but if `handle_401` fires before `__call__` resets it — or if `handle_401` is somehow reached when `num_401_calls` is still `None` — the expression `num_401_calls < 2` at line 180 raises `TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'NoneType' and 'int'` in Python 3. Even in the normal flow, the initial value of `None` (rather than `1`) is a hazard. The old code used `getattr(self, 'num_401_calls', 1)` as a safe fallback; the new code removed that safety net (line 177: `num_401_calls = self.tl.num_401_calls` with no default).

findings.title: `self.tl.num_401_calls += 1` will raise TypeError if `num_401_calls` is `None`
findings.detail: If `self.tl.num_401_calls` is `None` (its initial value from `init_per_thread_state`, line 78) when `handle_401` is entered, the check `num_401_calls < 2` on line 180 might pass (in Python 2, `None < 2` is True), and then `self.tl.num_401_calls += 1` on line 182 would fail with `TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +=: 'NoneType' and 'int'` in both Python versions. While normal usage always runs `__call__` (which sets `num_401_calls = 1`) before `handle_401` fires, the initialization value of `None` instead of a sensible default integer is fragile.

findings.title: `num_401_calls` reset to 1 happens after hook registration, not before — harmless but inconsistent with redirect handler
findings.detail: In `__call__`, `self.tl.num_401_calls = 1` (line 221) is set AFTER `r.register_hook('response', self.handle_401)` and `r.register_hook('response', self.handle_redirect)` (lines 219-220). If the response hooks could fire synchronously (they don't in practice, but the ordering is fragile), `handle_401` could read `num_401_calls` before it is set to 1. The original code initialized `num_401_calls` in `__init__` so it was always defined. Moving the reset to after hook registration, combined with the `None` initial value, is a correctness hazard.

findings.title: NoneType dereference / broken 401-loop guard when handle_401 is called before __call__
findings.detail: tl.num_401_calls is initialized to None in init_per_thread_state() (line 78). In __call__ it is only set to 1 at line 221, *after* the hooks are registered. If handle_401 or handle_redirect fires on a thread that has never executed __call__ (e.g. because the auth object's hooks were registered by another thread's call to r.register_hook and the response callback is dispatched on a different thread), `self.tl.num_401_calls` will still be None. The comparison `num_401_calls < 2` at line 180 will then raise a TypeError, and `self.tl.num_401_calls += 1` at line 182 will also fail. More concretely: because threading.local state is *per-thread*, the hook callbacks (handle_401, handle_redirect) run on whatever thread processes the response—which may be a *different* thread than the one that called __call__. That thread will have an un-initialized tl (no 'init' attribute), and `init_per_thread_state` is never called in the hook path, so every attribute access on self.tl in handle_401/handle_redirect will raise AttributeError, completely bypassing the 401-loop guard and making the authentication logic crash or be silently skipped depending on how the caller handles exceptions.

findings.title: Stale per-thread nonce/challenge state allows replaying captured nonces across requests
findings.detail: Because threading.local stores state permanently per thread (it is never cleared between requests on the same thread), a thread pulled from a pool that previously authenticated against *one server* will silently reuse tl.last_nonce, tl.chal, and tl.nonce_count for a *different* request made on the same thread (potentially to a different server or with a different auth object instance shared across sessions). The `init_per_thread_state` guard (`if not hasattr(self.tl, 'init')`) ensures initialization only happens once per thread, so there is no reset between requests on the same thread. An attacker who can influence thread scheduling (e.g. by timing requests) could observe the nonce reuse and use it to correlate or replay requests. The correct mitigation is to reset the per-thread state at the *start* of each request cycle (in __call__) rather than only when the thread is first seen.

findings.title: www-authenticate header parsed without strict validation — potential header injection via server-controlled input
findings.detail: The value of the `www-authenticate` header is taken from the server response and passed to `parse_dict_header` after a simple regex substitution. While this is not new to this PR, the PR preserves the pattern in the thread-local version. If `parse_dict_header` is vulnerable to specially crafted header values (e.g. containing newlines or quote escapes), a malicious server could inject values into tl.chal that influence subsequent Authorization header construction at line 152-162 (username, realm, nonce, uri, opaque, algorithm fields are interpolated into a string without escaping). This is worth reviewing in the context of the thread-local refactor because tl.chal is now thread-scoped and survives across requests on the same thread.

findings.title: Attribute name `tl` is too abbreviated and opaque
findings.detail: The existing code uses full, descriptive attribute names (`last_nonce`, `nonce_count`, `chal`, `pos`, `num_401_calls`). The name `tl` gives no hint that it is a thread-local storage object. A name like `_thread_local` or `_local` would be consistent with the surrounding verbosity and immediately communicate its purpose to readers.

findings.title: `init_per_thread_state` uses a sentinel attribute (`init`) that is misleadingly named
findings.detail: The sentinel `self.tl.init = True` uses the name `init` which reads like an action that has been taken, but it is really a boolean guard. Following the project's style of descriptive names, something like `_initialized` would be much clearer about its role as a one-time flag.

findings.title: Missing space around `=` in keyword assignment
findings.detail: `session.auth=auth` is missing spaces around the `=`. All other assignments in the test file (and the rest of the codebase) use `session.auth = auth` style.

findings.title: Test body not guarded at the top, leading to a silent no-op when `ThreadPool` is unavailable
findings.detail: The `if ThreadPool is not None:` guard only wraps the pool usage at the bottom of the test, meaning the test passes silently (exercising nothing) when `ThreadPool` cannot be imported. Other conditional-import patterns in the file (`try/except`) suggest that either the test should be skipped explicitly (e.g. `@pytest.mark.skipif`) or the guard placed at the very top so the intent is obvious. As written a reader must read to the end of the function to realise the test may be a no-op.

findings.title: `do_request` ignores its argument and returns a magic literal
findings.detail: The inner function `do_request(i)` never uses `i`, and returns the literal `1` which is never inspected. The unused parameter and the arbitrary return value add noise. Consistent with other test helpers in the file that return nothing, the `return 1` can simply be omitted, and if `i` is truly unused the parameter name could be `_` to signal that.

findings.title: init_per_thread_state(): no unit test for per-thread isolation of state attributes
findings.detail: The new `init_per_thread_state` method initialises `tl.last_nonce`, `tl.nonce_count`, `tl.chal`, `tl.pos`, and `tl.num_401_calls` in thread-local storage. The only new test (`test_DIGESTAUTH_THREADED`) fires 100 concurrent requests through httpbin and only asserts on the Authorization header value. It never verifies that two threads actually get independent state (e.g. different `last_nonce`, `nonce_count`, or `num_401_calls` values), so a regression where state leaks between threads could go undetected. A unit-level test that creates two threads, drives them through `__call__` / `handle_401` with mocked responses, and asserts that each thread's counter/nonce is independent is missing.

findings.title: init_per_thread_state(): 'already initialised' path (tl.init already set) is not tested
findings.detail: The guard `if not hasattr(self.tl, 'init')` means the initialisation block is skipped on the second call from the same thread. There is no test that calls `__call__` (which calls `init_per_thread_state`) twice from the same thread and asserts that state accumulated in the first call is preserved rather than reset. This branch could silently regress.

findings.title: handle_redirect(): thread-local num_401_calls reset on redirect not covered per-thread
findings.detail: `handle_redirect` now resets `self.tl.num_401_calls = 1`. No test exercises this code path with multiple concurrent threads each following a redirect, so the interaction between the redirect reset and per-thread state is untested.

findings.title: __call__(): tl.num_401_calls set to 1 at end — interaction with handle_401 counter not tested in isolation
findings.detail: `__call__` now sets `self.tl.num_401_calls = 1` unconditionally at the end (line 221). Previously `num_401_calls` started at 1 in `__init__` and was managed only via `handle_401`/`handle_redirect`. The new placement means every new request resets the counter to 1. There is no unit test (mocked, offline) that verifies this reset happens correctly when the same `HTTPDigestAuth` instance is reused across sequential or parallel calls, distinguishing it from the old attribute-on-self behaviour.

findings.title: test_DIGESTAUTH_THREADED silently skips when ThreadPool is unavailable
findings.detail: The entire threaded assertion block is inside `if ThreadPool is not None:`. If `multiprocessing.pool.ThreadPool` is unavailable (Python 2 environments or restricted environments), the test body is never executed and the suite passes vacuously. There is no `pytest.skip` or `@pytest.mark.skipif` so the skip is invisible, giving false confidence in CI.

findings.title: test_DIGESTAUTH_THREADED does not assert HTTP 200 status code
findings.detail: The test only checks `'"auth"' in r.request.headers['Authorization']` but does not assert `r.status_code == 200`. A concurrent authentication failure that still produces an Authorization header (e.g. a 401 response with a stale header from a previous attempt) would not be caught.

findings.title: No test for wrong-password path with concurrent threads
findings.detail: The existing `test_DIGESTAUTH_WRONG_HTTP_401_GET` tests the wrong-password 401 loop serially. There is no concurrent version, so the thread-safety of the `num_401_calls < 2` loop-prevention logic (the primary motivation for this PR) is not exercised under a wrong-credentials scenario.

findings.title: num_401_calls initialized to None and hook callbacks may run on a different thread — both cause crashes
findings.detail:
Two related critical issues stem from the thread-local design:

1. **None initialization**: In `init_per_thread_state` (line 78), `self.tl.num_401_calls` is set to `None`. The original code used `getattr(self, 'num_401_calls', 1)` as a safe fallback; the new code removes that safety net. The initial value should be `1` (not `None`) and/or the reset should occur before hook registration.

2. **Cross-thread callback dispatch**: Because `threading.local` state is per-thread, `handle_401` and `handle_redirect` must run on the same thread that called `__call__`. The design assumption that the calling thread and callback thread are the same must be explicitly documented or enforced.

findings.detail: The `if ThreadPool is not None:` guard wraps only the pool usage at the bottom of the test function, so when `multiprocessing.pool.ThreadPool` cannot be imported the test passes vacuously without exercising anything. There is no `pytest.skip` or `@pytest.mark.skipif`, making the skip invisible in CI and providing false confidence. Either move the guard to the very top of the test or replace it with an explicit `pytest.skip` so the absence of `ThreadPool` is surfaced.
